claim: Adding Cr mitigates intergranular coarse κ*-carbide precipitation in γ-austenite, which is beneficial for elongation.
evidence_text: Adding Cr mitigates intergranular coarse $\kappa ^ { * } .$ -carbide precipitation in γ-austenite, which is beneficial for elongation [33–35].

claim: Four ingots with nominal compositions Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C-xNiyCr (x = 0, 2 wt%; y = 0, 3, 5 wt%) were prepared by vacuum induction melting.
evidence_text: Four ingots with nominal compositions of Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C-xNiyCr (x = 0, 2 wt%, y = 0, 3, 5 wt%) were prepared by vacuum induction melting under a high-purity Ar atmosphere.

claim: As given in Table 1, the measured compositions (wt%) are: 3Cr steel Fe-20.1Mn-8.21Al-1.31C-0.18Ni-2.88Cr; 2Ni steel Fe-21.0Mn-9.35Al-1.23C-2.05Ni-0.05Cr; 2Ni3Cr steel Fe-20.3Mn-8.63Al-1.41C-2.11Ni-2.89Cr; 2Ni5Cr steel Fe-20.8Mn-9.09Al-1.34C-2.08Ni-4.90Cr.
evidence_text: The measured compositions of these as-cast ingots are shown in Table 1. Table 1 Measured chemical compositions (wt.%) of the four Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C-xNi-yCr (x = 0, 2 wt%, y = 0, 3, 5 wt%) steels.

claim: The as-cast ingots were homogenized at 1200 °C for 2 h, hot-rolled from 1040–1060 °C to 950–970 °C to 4 mm thick sheets with 95% thickness reduction, and then online ultrafast cooled to ambient temperature at ~40 °C/s.
evidence_text: The as-cast ingots were homogenized at 1200 °C for 2 h. The initial and finishing rolling temperature is 1040-1060 °C and 950-970 °C, respectively. The ingots were hot-rolled (HR) to 4 mm-thick sheets with 95% thickness reduction. The plate was online ultrafast cooled (UFCed) to ambient temperature with a cooling rate of ~40 °C/s.

claim: TEM specimens were electropolished using 10 vol% perchloric acid and 90 vol% ethanol electrolyte at 20 V and -30°C after grinding to <80 μm.
evidence_text: The TEM specimens were cut by EDM with a thickness of 1 mm and then first ground into foils with a thickness of <80 μm. Subsequently, the foils were further thinned using Struers TenuPol-3 electrochemical double-jet equipment, which was formed into an electrolyte of 10 vol% perchloric acid and 90 vol% ethanol at a voltage of 20 V and a temperature of -30 °C.

claim: In 3Cr and 2Ni5Cr steels, dark-field TEM images reveal homogeneously distributed nanosized LRO domains; SADPs show faint superlattice reflections at 1/2{0-22}γ or 1/2{200}γ without satellite diffraction spots, indicating LRO domains rather than κ′-carbides.
evidence_text: Fig. 1i and l are dark-field (DF) TEM images revealing nanosized long range ordering (LRO) domains homogeneously distributed in the γ matrix. ... Very faint superlattice reflections can be seen at the 1/2{0-22}γ or/and 1/2{200}γ positions, and satellite diffraction spots around the primary γ diffraction spots cannot be found, indicating the formation of LRO domains in the online UFCed 3Cr and 2Ni5Cr steels. No satellite diffraction spots around γ diffraction spots and high carbon content in Fe–Mn–Al–C lightweight steels illustrate the formation of the κ-carbides/LRO domains rather than κ′-carbides [14,39].

claim: IFFT images using superlattice reflections show that LRO domains in 3Cr and 2Ni5Cr are ellipsoidal with an interconnected morphology typical of spinodal decomposition; the average sizes are 1.75 nm (3Cr) and 2.14 nm (2Ni5Cr), while κ-carbide sizes are 10.69 nm (2Ni) and 5.46 nm (2Ni3Cr).
evidence_text: As shown in Fig. 3b and h, the microstructure of LRO domains is ellipsoidal, and the spatially localized LRO domains have an interconnected morphology, which is often observed in spinodal decomposition. The average sizes of the LRO domains in 3Cr steel and 2Ni5Cr steel measured using IFFT images are approximately 1.75 nm and 2.14 nm, respectively. The average sizes of these LRO domains and κ-carbides determined by measuring more than 200 domains by Image-Pro Plus software are plotted in Fig. 3i. Their average sizes decrease from ~10.69 nm through ~5.46 nm and ~2.14 nm–~1.75 nm for the 2Ni steel, 2Ni3Cr steel, 2Ni5Cr steel and 3Cr steel.

claim: Fig. 1 presents EBSD IPF and phase maps (a–d), TEM bright-field images (e–h), dark-field images (i–l), and SADPs along [011]γ zone axis (m–p) for the four steels.
evidence_text: Fig. (1a -1d ) and Fig. (1a -1d ) are EBSD inverse pole figures (IPFs) and EBSD phase maps showing the microstructure of the 3Cr, 2Ni, 2Ni3Cr and 2Ni5Cr steels, respectively. ... (e, f, g, h) TEM bright-field images. (i, j, k, l) TEM dark-field images taken from the {110} LRO or {110} κ reflections. (m, n, 0, p) Selected area diffraction patterns recorded along the [001] γ zone axis

claim: In UFCed Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C steels with equivalent grain sizes, the 2Ni5Cr steel exhibits the highest total elongation, with an increase of approximately 16% compared to the Ni-containing steel without Cr (2Ni steel).
evidence_text: the highest total elongation was found in the 2Ni5Cr steel with an increment of ~16% for comparison with no Cr steel (i.e., 2Ni steel).

claim: The addition of Ni increases the yield strength: compared to the 3Cr steel, the 2Ni3Cr steel shows a yield strength increase of approximately 141 MPa.
evidence_text: Compared to 3Cr steel, steel with a higher Ni content (i.e., 2Ni3Cr steel) has a yield strength increase of ~141 MPa.

claim: In the 2Ni, 2Ni3Cr, and 2Ni5Cr Fe–20Mn–9Al-1.5C steels, increasing Cr content decreases yield strength from 1202 MPa to 1071 MPa and tensile strength from 1283 MPa to 1187 MPa, while total elongation increases from 34.1% to 54.5%. The 3Cr steel without Ni achieves a total elongation of up to 54.4%.
evidence_text: the yield strength decreases from 1202 MPa to 1071 MPa with the addition of Cr , and the tensile strength decreases from 1283 MPa to 1187 MPa. However, the addition of Cr in 2Ni, 2Ni3Cr and 2Ni5Cr steels increases the total elongation from 34.1% to 54.5%, and the total elongation of 3Cr steels without added Ni is up to 54.4%.

claim: In 3Cr steel at 3% engineering strain, dislocation slip exhibits strong planarity; the [011](1-11) slip system has a Schmid factor of 0.52, and dislocation multipoles are localized within {111} slip bands with interaction between (1-11) and (11-1) slip bands.
evidence_text: When the strain reaches 3%, dislocation slip is the main feature, while the [011](1-11) slip system in the grain has a Schmid factor of 0.52. The dislocation slip has a strong planarity, such as dislocation multipoles localized within {111} slip bands (Fig. 6a), dislocations constrained within planar (1-11) slip bands and restricted by the planar slip bands (Fig. 6b) and the interaction of (1-11) and (11-1) slip bands from different slip systems (Fig. 6c).

claim: In 3Cr steel near fracture (54% strain), high-density dislocations and very fine slip bands are observed; a dozen nanometre-width deformation twins appear only near the fracture surface and contribute little to ductility, with strain hardenability mainly contributed by HDDA densification and dynamic slip band refinement (DSBR).
evidence_text: High-density dislocations and very fine slip bands can be observed (Fig. 6g–h), and a dozen nanometre-width deformation twins are seen in the bright-field image (Fig. 6i). Since the deformation twins appear only in the vicinity of the fracture surfaces, their contribution to the overall ductility and strainhardening capacity is expected to be very limited. This implies that 3Cr steels are dominated by HDDA and dislocation slip during deformation, and their strain hardenability is mainly contributed by HDDA densification and dynamic slip band refinement (DSBR).

claim: In 2Ni steel at 10% engineering strain, planar slip bands multiply and refine, forming three groups of slip systems: [011](1-11), [011](−111), and [011](200).
evidence_text: As the strain accumulates to 10%, the microstructural evolution can be seen in Fig. 7c–g and is characterized by the multiplication of planar slip bands as well as the gradual refinement space between the slip band (Fig. 7c–f) and the (200) slip band, forming three groups of slip systems: [011](1-11), [011](−111) and [011](200) (Fig. 7g).

claim: In 2Ni steel close to fracture, dislocation plane slip penetrates the grain and cuts through the κ-carbides (Fig. 8).
evidence_text: the near-fracture state of 2Ni steel was also found to exhibit dislocation plane slip after deformation under loading, and the plane slip penetrated the grain and cut through the κ⁃carbides.

claim: First-principles DFT (GGA-PBE) calculations give the following formation energies: ideal κ-carbide (Fe,Mn)₃AlC -0.160 eV/atom, Cr-rich Cr₂₁(FeC₃)₂ -0.049 eV/atom, Ni-rich Fe₂NiC 0.082 eV/atom, and Ni-rich AlNi₃C -0.181 eV/atom (Table 3).
evidence_text: The ideal stoichiometry of κ-carbides is (Fe, Mn) AlC [29]. The results of the first-principle calculations for the possible formation of κ-carbides structure are shown in Table 3. κ-carbides (Fe, Mn)_3AlC -0.160, Cr-rich carbides Cr_21(FeC_3)_2 -0.049, Ni-rich carbides Fe_2NiC 0.082, AlNi_3C -0.181.

claim: Cr addition increases the formation energy and nucleation energy barrier of κ-carbides, suppressing κ-carbide nucleation and growth and reducing κ-carbide size; Cr atoms substitute for Al forming (Fe,Mn)CrC, which increases interfacial and elastic strain energies.
evidence_text: With the addition of the Cr element into the κ-carbides, the formation energy of the κ-carbides increases, requiring greater energy for phase transition, thus making it more difficult for κ-carbides nucleation and growth. Thus, the size of the κ-carbides decreases. … And Cr atoms take the place of Al atoms to form (Fe, Mn) CrC [54]. This also increases the formation energy of κ-carbides, as well as the interfacial energies and elastic strain energies between κ-carbides and the austenite matrix, causing an increase in the nucleation energy barrier of κ-carbides.

claim: Ni addition reduces the formation energy of κ-carbides (AlNi₃C structure, -0.181 eV/atom), lowers the nucleation barrier, and promotes κ-carbide formation, leading to larger κ-carbide size; Ni atoms (radius 1.24 Å) preferentially displace Fe (1.27 Å) and Mn (1.32 Å) to form Ni-rich κ-carbides.
evidence_text: since Ni has an atomic radius of 1.24◦A, Fe atoms have a radius of 1.27◦A, and Mn atoms have a radius of 1.32◦A. They have equivalent atomic radii, and Ni atoms preferentially displace Fe/Mn atoms to form Ni-rich κ-carbides with AlNi C structure (with a formation energy of − 0.181 eV/atom). The addition of Ni atoms in κ-carbide reduces the formation energy of κ-carbide, lowers the critical formal energy of κ-carbide, and promotes the formation of κ-carbide. The reduction in the nucleation energy barrier is the main reason for increasing the size of κ-carbide in Ni-containing lightweight steels.

claim: The Fe₂NiC structure has a positive formation energy of 0.082 eV/atom, making it unlikely to form in nature.
evidence_text: Since the nucleation energy of the Fe NiC structure is > 0, it is less likely for the Fe_2NiC structure of the κ-carbides to form in nature.

claim: First-principles calculations give formation energies of (Fe,Mn)₃AlC κ-carbide as −0.160 eV/atom, Cr₂₁(FeC₃)₂ as −0.049 eV/atom, Fe₂NiC as +0.082 eV/atom, and AlNi₃C as −0.181 eV/atom.
evidence_text: κ-carbides $(Fe, Mn)_3AlC$ -0.160 eV/atom … Cr-rich carbides $Cr_{21}(FeC_3)_2$ -0.049 eV/atom … Ni-rich carbides $Fe_2NiC$ 0.082 eV/atom … $AlNi_3C$ -0.181 eV/atom

claim: The yield strength of the Fe–Mn–Al–C steels is decomposed into frictional stress (σ₀), solid solution strengthening (σss), grain boundary strengthening (σgb), and precipitation strengthening (σprep) as σys = σ₀ + σss + σgb + σprep.
evidence_text: The yield strength can be represented as a linear combination of strengthening mechanisms, as illustrated in Equation (1): σys = σ0 + σss + σgb + σprep

claim: Using τ₀ = 2×10⁻⁴G with the shear modulus G = 70 GPa for Fe–Mn–Al–C austenite, the frictional stress σ₀ = 3τ₀ is calculated as 42 MPa.
evidence_text: Given that the G value for Fe–Mn–Al–C steel is 70 GPa, σ0 here is calculated to be 42 MPa.

claim: Grain boundary strengthening is estimated by σgb = KHP·deff⁻⁰·⁵ with KHP = 446.8 MPa·μm⁰·⁵. Using twin-containing grain sizes deff of 3.51, 3.73, 3.72, and 2.2 μm for 3Cr, 2Ni, 2Ni3Cr, and 2Ni5Cr, the calculated σgb values are 238.5, 231.3, 231.7, and 301.2 MPa, respectively.
evidence_text: KHP represents the Hall-Petch coefficient for steels in this study (KHP = 446.8 MPa μm0.5) … deff values from EBSD analysis as 3.51, 3.73, 3.72, and 2.2 μm, respectively … the calculated strengthening component results … 238.5, 231.3, 231.7, and 301.2 MPa, respectively.

claim: Solid solution strengthening follows the empirical formula σss = 279[C] − 1.5[Mn] + 20.5[Al] + 2.9[Ni] + 3.9[Cr] (wt.%), giving values of 515.4 MPa (3Cr), 509.5 MPa (2Ni), 557.2 MPa (2Ni3Cr), and 554.1 MPa (2Ni5Cr).
evidence_text: σss = 279[C] - 1.5[Mn] + 20.5[Al] + 2.9[Ni] + 3.9[Cr] … solute strengthening components for the 3Cr, 2Ni, 2Ni3Cr, and 2Ni5Cr samples are 515.4, 509.5, 557.2 and 554.1 MPa, respectively.

claim: Precipitation strengthening (σprep) is dominated by dislocation shearing of κ-carbides, combining ordering strengthening (σorder), coherency strengthening (σcoh), and modulus mismatch strengthening (σmod); the larger of σorder and σcoh+σmod is taken as σprep.
evidence_text: moving dislocations shear the precipitates, thus, shear strengthening is the predominant mechanism. The shearing mechanism involves three strengthening processes: ordering strengthening (σorder), coherency strengthening (σcoh), and modulus mismatch strengthening (σmod). … we assume that the larger of the two factors (σorder and σcoh+σmod) contributes to the precipitation strengthening.

claim: In 3Cr and 2Ni5Cr steels, some LRO nanodomains are smaller than the critical size, causing σorder to also contribute to precipitation strengthening, whereas in other samples σcoh+σmod dominates.
evidence_text: in the 3Cr and 2Ni5Cr samples with LRO nanodomains, σorder also contributes to σprep because some of the nanodomains are smaller than the critical size.

claim: Figure 10 shows the calculated precipitation hardening (σprep) as a function of precipitate radius for (a) 3Cr, (b) 2Ni, (c) 2Ni3Cr, and (d) 2Ni5Cr steels, with the red dashed line marking the transition radius where the dominant mechanism switches from σorder to σcoh+σmod.
evidence_text: The calculated σprep is shown in Fig. 10, where the red dashed line represents the transition radius, at which the dominant strengthening mechanism shifts from σorder to σcoh+σmod. For the 3Cr, 2Ni, 2Ni3Cr and 2Ni5Cr steels, the strength contribution of σcoh+σmod is dominant.

claim: Figure 11 compares the sum of the four strengthening contributions (σprep, σgb, σss, σ₀) with experimental tensile yield strengths for 3Cr, 2Ni, 2Ni3Cr, and 2Ni5Cr, showing very good agreement and confirming the decomposition model.
evidence_text: Fig. 11 compares the results with the measured yield strengths, illustrating the contribution to the yield strength of each strengthening mechanism for each sample. There is a correlation between the calculated and experimental values of yield strength … which are in very good agreement.

claim: Solid solution strengthening is the primary contributor to yield strength, while precipitation strengthening is significant; synergistic Ni and Cr alloying increases the size of the precipitate phase, leading to a gradual increase in precipitation strengthening with precipitate growth.
evidence_text: Solution strengthening is evidently the primary strengthening mechanism for these samples, with the enhancement in yield strength … closely associated with a carbon content as high as 1.5%. Precipitation strengthening also plays a significant role … the contribution from Ni and Cr elements synergistically … providing an increase in the size of the precipitate phase, leading to a gradual increase in precipitation strengthening as the precipitate size grows.

claim: With further strain increase, the microstructure of 3Cr steel in stage III is characterized by mutual annihilation and cross-slip of dislocations, refinement of slip bands, and the occurrence of stacking faults and deformation twins only at very high strain levels near ultimate failure.
evidence_text: With a further increase in strain, the microstructural evolution is characterized by mutual annihilation and cross-slip through sliding dislocations (Fig. 6e–f and Fig. 12b) and by refinement of the slip bands (Figs. 6d and 12b). ... It should also be noted that stacking fault and deformation twins also occur in 3Cr steels (Fig. 7i–l and Fig. 12c), but only at very high strain levels near ultimate failure.

claim: Compared to 3Cr steel, the strain hardening stage in 2Ni steel is shortened; at stage II the strain hardening rate increases with strain, and at stage III accelerated dynamic recovery occurs, along with a small number of stacking faults near final failure.
evidence_text: It results that strain hardening rate increase with the strain increasing, and the strain hardening stage is shortened than 3Cr steel. At strain-hardening Stage II, the strain-hardening rate increases with increasing strain. When entering strain-hardening Stage III, it is expected to be due to accelerated dynamic recovery and hard-to-dynamic slip band refinement (Fig. 7i). ... A small number of stack faults were found at very high strain levels close to final failure (Fig. 7j–l and Fig. 12e).
